questions.question: Explain the difference between guided and unguided transmission media with examples.
keyPoints: Guided media: physical cables (twisted pair, coaxial, fiber) - signals confined to specific path
Unguided media: wireless (radio, microwave, infrared, satellite) - signals propagate through air/space
Examples: Guided - Ethernet cables, fiber optic links; Unguided - WiFi, cellular, satellite TV

questions.question: Describe the structure of a fiber optic cable and its advantages over copper cables.
keyPoints: Structure: Core (glass/plastic), Cladding (reflective layer), Buffer coating, Outer jacket
Advantages: Higher bandwidth, lower attenuation, immune to EMI, better security, lighter weight, longer distances without repeaters

questions.question: What is attenuation? How can it be overcome in data transmission?
keyPoints: Attenuation: loss of signal strength during transmission due to distance and medium properties
Overcome by: Using repeaters to regenerate signal, amplifiers for analog signals, choosing lower-loss media like fiber, using proper termination to minimize reflections

questions.question: Explain the different categories of UTP cables used in networking.
keyPoints: Cat 3: Up to 10 Mbps, 16 MHz, voice/10Base-T
Cat 5: Up to 100 Mbps, 100 MHz, 100Base-TX
Cat 5e: Up to 1 Gbps, 100 MHz, 1000Base-T
Cat 6: Up to 10 Gbps, 250 MHz, 10GBase-T
Cat 6a: Up to 10 Gbps, 500 MHz, better shielding

questions.question: Compare single-mode and multi-mode fiber optic cables.
keyPoints: Core diameter: Single-mode (8-10μm), Multi-mode (50-62.5μm)
Distance: Single-mode (up to 100km), Multi-mode (up to 2km)
Light source: Single-mode (laser), Multi-mode (LED)
Cost: Single-mode more expensive but cheaper long-term
Applications: Single-mode (telecom/backbone), Multi-mode (LANs/data centers)

questions.question: What are the factors to consider when choosing transmission media for a network?
keyPoints: 1. Bandwidth requirements (data rate needs)
2. Distance (coverage area)
3. Cost (budget constraints)
4. EMI susceptibility (environment)
5. Installation complexity
6. Security needs
7. Future scalability
8. Environmental factors (indoor/outdoor)

questions.question: Explain the working principle of microwave and satellite communication.
keyPoints: Microwave: Uses high-frequency radio waves (1-300 GHz), requires line-of-sight, point-to-point communication, used for telephone/data networks
Satellite: Uses orbiting satellites to relay signals, geostationary or low-earth orbit, used for TV/internet/telephone, covers large geographic areas
Advantages: Long distance, no ground infrastructure needed; Disadvantages: Latency, weather interference, high cost

questions.question: Describe the types of guided transmission media in detail with their characteristics, applications, and limitations.
keyPoints: 1. Twisted Pair: UTP/STP, categories, EMI susceptibility, Ethernet applications, limited distance (100m)
2. Coaxial: Thicknet/Thinnet, better shielding than twisted pair, cable TV (CATV), higher bandwidth, longer distances (500m)
3. Fiber Optic: Single/Multi-mode, light-based transmission, highest bandwidth, immunity to EMI, security, applications in backbone networks, limitations: cost, installation complexity
Compare: Fiber best for long-distance/high-speed, coaxial for cable TV/broadcast, twisted pair for LANs

questions.question: Explain the various unguided (wireless) transmission media and their applications in communication systems.
keyPoints: Radio Waves: Low to high frequency, omnidirectional, penetrates walls, used in WiFi, Bluetooth, cellular
Microwave: Line-of-sight, point-to-point, high bandwidth, used in telephone networks, satellite up/down links
Infrared: Line-of-sight only, short-range, used in TV remotes, cannot penetrate walls, low cost
Satellite: GEO/MEO/LEO orbits, wide coverage, used for TV, internet, telephone, weather monitoring
Each has different frequency bands, advantages, limitations based on distance, data rate, line-of-sight requirements

questions.question: Discuss the factors affecting signal quality in transmission media and the measures to minimize signal degradation.
keyPoints: Factors: Attenuation (signal loss), EMI (electromagnetic interference), Crosstalk (adjacent channel interference), Noise (thermal/shot noise), Bandwidth limitations, Reflection/standing waves
Measures: Use shielded cables (STP, coaxial), proper grounding, maintain impedance matching, use repeaters/amplifiers, twisted pair reduces crosstalk, fiber optic eliminates EMI
Performance metrics: Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR), Bit Error Rate (BER), bandwidth-delay product
Design considerations: Choose appropriate medium for environment, install away from EMI sources, use proper connectors and termination

questions.question: Compare and contrast the different types of cables used in LANs with respect to speed, distance, cost, and applications.
keyPoints: UTP Cat 5e: 1 Gbps, 100m, lowest cost, common in offices/home
UTP Cat 6/6a: 10 Gbps (Cat 6a up to 100m), 100m, moderate cost, modern installations
STP: Similar to UTP but better shielding, higher cost, industrial/EMI-prone areas
Coaxial: 10 Mbps (Thinnet 185m, Thicknet 500m), older LANs, cable TV distribution
Fiber Optic: 10-100+ Gbps, km range (single-mode), highest cost, backbone/enterprise networks
Selection criteria: Based on network requirements, budget, environment, future needs
